Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: ISRO is India's national space agency.
Geostationary satellites orbit at an altitude of approximately 35,786 km above the Earth's equator.
GSLV Mk III (LVM3) is ISRO's heaviest launch vehicle.
Communication satellites play a crucial role in DTH, VSAT, and telecommunication services.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of advanced communication satellites like GSAT-25 for India's digital infrastructure and socio-economic development.
Analyze how ISRO's indigenous capabilities in space technology contribute to India's strategic autonomy and global standing.
Evaluate the role of space-based services in bridging the digital divide and enabling initiatives like Digital India and BharatNet.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India's space sector in the coming decade, including private sector participation and international collaborations.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the GSAT-25 satellite orbiting Earth, with communication signals radiating.
GS3.trivia: India launched its first satellite, Aryabhata, on April 19, 1975, with the help of the Soviet Union.

GS3.headline: MSP Policy Debate Intensifies Amidst Calls for Legal Guarantee and Market Reforms
GS3.explainer:
• The debate over Minimum Support Price (MSP) policy for agricultural produce intensified, with farmer organizations demanding a legal guarantee for MSP.
• The government reiterated its commitment to farmer welfare but emphasized the need for market-oriented reforms to ensure sustainable agricultural growth.
• Economists highlighted the fiscal implications of a legally guaranteed MSP and suggested alternative mechanisms like deficiency payments or price stabilization funds.
• Discussions included diversification of crops, improving storage and logistics infrastructure, and reducing post-harvest losses to empower farmers.
• The issue remains a critical point of contention between government policy and farmer demands, impacting agricultural policy direction.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System—objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
keyTerms: Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP)
prelimsPoints: MSP is announced by the government for 22 mandated crops and Fair and Remunerative Price (FRP) for sugarcane.
CACP recommends MSPs based on A2+FL (all paid out costs plus family labour) and C2 (comprehensive cost).
MSP aims to protect farmers from excessive fall in prices during bumper production years.
Swaminathan Commission recommended MSP at 50% above C2 cost.
mainsPoints: Critically examine the rationale behind the demand for a legal guarantee for Minimum Support Price (MSP) and its potential implications for India's agricultural economy.
Discuss the various arguments for and against a legally guaranteed MSP, considering fiscal burden, market distortions, and farmer welfare.
Propose alternative policy mechanisms and reforms that can ensure fair prices for farmers while promoting sustainable and efficient agricultural markets.
Analyze the role of agricultural infrastructure, diversification, and value addition in enhancing farmer incomes and reducing dependence on MSP.
GS3.imageDescription: Farmers protesting with placards demanding legal guarantee for MSP.
GS3.trivia: The concept of MSP was introduced in the mid-1960s as part of the Green Revolution strategy to incentivize food grain production.

GS3.headline: India Advocates for Equitable Climate Finance at G20 Ministerial Meeting
GS3.explainer:
• India strongly advocated for a substantial increase in climate finance from developed nations at the G20 Environment and Climate Sustainability Ministerial Meeting.
• The Indian delegation highlighted the historical responsibility of developed countries and the need for accessible, affordable, and predictable finance for developing nations' climate action.
• Discussions included the operationalization of the Loss and Damage Fund and mechanisms for technology transfer to support climate adaptation and mitigation efforts.
• India reiterated its commitment to its Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) but stressed that climate justice requires adequate financial support.
• The meeting concluded with a joint statement acknowledging the urgency of climate action and the need for multilateral cooperation, though specific finance commitments remained contentious.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: G20 is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union (EU).
Developed countries pledged $100 billion per year in climate finance by 2020, which has not been fully met.
Loss and Damage Fund was established at COP27 in Sharm El Sheikh.

India's NDCs include reducing emissions intensity by 45% by 2030 from 2005 levels and achieving about 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy resources by 2030.
mainsPoints: Analyze the concept of climate justice and its relevance in the context of global climate negotiations, particularly regarding climate finance.
Discuss India's stance on climate finance at international forums like the G20 and its rationale for demanding greater contributions from developed nations.
Evaluate the effectiveness of existing climate finance mechanisms and the challenges in mobilizing adequate and accessible funds for developing countries.
Suggest policy approaches for fostering greater international cooperation on climate action, including technology transfer and capacity building.
GS3.imageDescription: Leaders from G20 nations discussing climate change around a conference table.
GS3.trivia: The G20 was formed in 1999 in response to the Asian financial crisis.

GS3.headline: India Achieves Milestone in Indigenous Vaccine Development for Tropical Diseases
GS3.explainer:
• Indian researchers and pharmaceutical companies announced a significant breakthrough in developing an indigenous multi-valent vaccine for several prevalent tropical diseases.
• The vaccine, developed through public-private partnerships, has shown promising results in late-stage clinical trials, offering hope for widespread prevention.
• This achievement underscores India's growing capabilities in biotechnology and pharmaceutical research, contributing to global health security.
• The government emphasized its commitment to affordable and accessible healthcare, indicating plans for mass production and distribution of the vaccine.
• The development positions India as a key player in vaccine innovation, especially for diseases endemic to tropical and subtropical regions.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- Developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: India is known as the 'pharmacy of the world' due to its large-scale vaccine and generic drug production.
National Biopharma Mission aims to accelerate biopharmaceutical development.
Diseases like Dengue, Chikungunya, Malaria, and Zika are prevalent tropical diseases.
The Department of Biotechnology (DBT) supports research in this area.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of indigenous vaccine development for tropical diseases in enhancing India's public health security and global health leadership.
Analyze the role of public-private partnerships and government support in fostering innovation in India's biotechnology and pharmaceutical sectors.
Evaluate the challenges in making vaccines affordable and accessible to all sections of the population, particularly in remote areas.
Examine how such breakthroughs contribute to achieving Universal Health Coverage and addressing the disease burden in developing countries.
GS3.imageDescription: Scientists in a laboratory examining vaccine samples, with advanced equipment.
GS3.trivia: Edward Jenner developed the world's first vaccine for smallpox in 1796.

GS3.headline: Editorial: Economic Liberalization in India - Three Decades On, A Mixed Legacy
GS3.explainer:
• This editorial reflects on the over three decades since India embarked on its path of economic liberalization in 1991, analyzing its profound impact.
• It acknowledges the significant economic growth, poverty reduction, and integration into the global economy facilitated by these reforms.
• However, it also critically examines the mixed legacy, pointing to rising income inequality, jobless growth, and regional disparities that have emerged or intensified.
• The article discusses the need for 'next-generation reforms' focusing on inclusive growth, human capital development, and environmental sustainability.
• It concludes that while liberalization laid the foundation for a modern economy, addressing its downsides is crucial for India's future trajectory.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key drivers and initial impact of India's economic liberalization reforms initiated in 1991.
Critically evaluate the 'mixed legacy' of these reforms, highlighting both their successes (e.g., growth, trade) and failures (e.g., inequality, jobless growth, regional disparities).
Discuss the concept of 'next-generation reforms' required for India to achieve inclusive and sustainable development in the coming decades.
Examine how the Indian economy has transformed over the past three decades due to liberalization and what challenges remain in its integration with the global economy.
GS3.imageDescription: A timeline graphic depicting major economic milestones in India since 1991.
GS3.trivia: The 1991 economic reforms were introduced by then Finance Minister Dr. Manmohan Singh under Prime Minister P.V. Narasimha Rao.

GS3.headline: Mumbai Trans Harbour Sea Link Phase 2 Inauguration Set to Boost Regional Connectivity
GS3.explainer:
• The second phase of the Mumbai Trans Harbour Sea Link (MTHL), also known as Atal Setu, is slated for inauguration, significantly enhancing connectivity in the Mumbai Metropolitan Region.
• This phase extends the sea link further, reducing travel time between Mumbai and Navi Mumbai and facilitating faster access to the Navi Mumbai International Airport.
• The mega-infrastructure project is expected to spur economic development in the extended Mumbai region, including the Jawaharlal Nehru Port (JNPT) area.
• It incorporates advanced engineering techniques and environmental mitigation measures to minimize ecological impact.
• MTHL is a crucial component of India's broader national infrastructure development push under the Gati Shakti Master Plan.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
prelimsPoints: MTHL is India's longest sea bridge.
Connects Sewri in Mumbai to Nhava Sheva in Navi Mumbai.
Named 'Atal Bihari Vajpayee Smruti Nhava Sheva Atal Setu'.
Aims to decongest Mumbai and boost development in Navi Mumbai.
mainsPoints: Discuss the socio-economic impact of mega-infrastructure projects like the Mumbai Trans Harbour Sea Link on urban development and regional connectivity.
Analyze how such projects align with national development strategies like the Gati Shakti Master Plan and contribute to multimodal connectivity.
Evaluate the engineering challenges and environmental considerations involved in constructing large-scale sea links.
Examine the potential for infrastructure development to drive economic growth and job creation in metropolitan regions.
GS3.imageDescription: An aerial view of the majestic Mumbai Trans Harbour Sea Link stretching across the bay.
GS3.trivia: The Bandra-Worli Sea Link, inaugurated in 2009, was India's first cable-stayed bridge over the open sea.

GS3.headline: Smart Cities Mission Achieves Key Milestones in Sustainable Urbanization
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs announced that the Smart Cities Mission has achieved significant milestones, completing over 75% of its proposed projects.
• Key successes include integrated command and control centers, smart mobility solutions, and improved waste management systems in selected cities.
• The mission has demonstrated the potential for technology-driven urban planning to enhance liveability, sustainability, and economic growth.
• Challenges persist in funding, inter-agency coordination, and ensuring equitable access to smart infrastructure for all residents.
• The government is now focusing on scaling up successful models and integrating lessons learned into future urban development policies.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: Smart Cities Mission was launched in 2015.
Aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure, a clean and sustainable environment, and a decent quality of life to their citizens.
100 cities were selected under the mission.
Integrated Command and Control Centres (ICCCs) are the brain for city operations.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and achievements of the Smart Cities Mission in promoting sustainable urbanization in India.
Discuss the role of technology and integrated command and control centers in improving urban governance and service delivery.
Evaluate the challenges faced by the Smart Cities Mission, including funding constraints, citizen participation, and equitable development.
Suggest policy recommendations for scaling up successful smart city models and ensuring inclusive urban development in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A futuristic cityscape with smart infrastructure, green spaces, and integrated technology.
GS3.trivia: The concept of 'smart city' originated in the early 2000s, often linked with the rise of information and communication technologies (ICT).
